title: NEW RESEARCH REVEALS HIDDEN SCALE OF CHILD HOMELESSNESS
full_text:
Poverty
More than 33,000 children and young people in Aotearoa are growing up in severe housing deprivation, with new research showing they are significantly more likely to miss school, suffer preventable illness and come into contact with the justice system.
They are also 2.9 times more likely to experience abuse than children living in stable homes.
The Coalition to End Women’s Homelessness, which commissioned the research, says it shows the profound impact that housing instability is having on children across Aotearoa.
And they say finding a housing solution for women - particularly mothers - is an essential part of the solution.
“These are babies, toddlers, and young people who are carrying the weight of housing insecurity at a critical stage of their development, with consequences that can shape the rest of their lives,” says Dr Jo Cribb, Chair of Wellington Homeless Women’s Trust Te Whare Nukunoa.
The report by analytics firm Taylor Fry drew on data from the 2023 Census to map the hidden scale of child homelessness across the country.
It found 22,788 children, were in uninhabitable housing - a further 7767 were crowded into someone else’s home, 1974 were in temporary accommodation and 663 had no shelter at all.
The effects of the housing instability extended into the classroom with children experiencing severe housing deprivation 1.4 times more likely to be absent during the school year and 2.3 times more likely to have been suspended in the previous five years. The suspension rate was 1.7%, compared with 0.74% for other children.
They were also 3.2 times more likely to have experienced a placement with Oranga Tamariki than children in the comparison group.
The research found there were also strong intergenerational patterns. More than a quarter, 26.39%, of children experiencing severe housing deprivation had a parent who had been charged with a criminal offence in the previous five years, compared with 11.8% of other children meaning they were 3.2 times more likely to have had a parent in prison during the same period.
Cribb said the findings pointed to systems failing to act early enough and that solutions needed to include mothers.
“You cannot separate children experiencing homelessness from women’s homelessness. When mothers don’t have access to safe, stable housing, their children are directly impacted.”
For the youngest children, the consequences were acute. Among those under five, homeless children were twice as likely to have never been enrolled with a Primary Health Organisation, 7.7% compared with 3.9% of other children under five. PHO enrolment is a standard proxy for engagement with the healthcare system and a common pathway to preventative care.
Nearly half, 48.3%, of homeless children under five had never been enrolled in early childhood education, compared with 30.4% of other children in that age group. The report notes this data does not include Ngā Kōhanga Reo or most licensed playgroups, a significant limitation for a cohort that the research found was disproportionately Māori.
The health gap for under-fives was also reflected in hospitalisation rates - those experiencing severe housing deprivation were 1.5 times more likely to have been diagnosed with a preventable respiratory condition, 2.6 times more likely to have been diagnosed with a vaccine-preventable disease, and 2.4 times more likely to have a preventable dermatological condition.
These are potentially avoidable hospitalisations that could be reduced through primary care, public health interventions or housing policy, the report says.
The findings were most concentrated in Auckland, Northland and Gisborne, and children experiencing severe housing deprivation were more likely to be Māori or Pacific. The report notes the Gisborne result may have been affected by Cyclone Gabrielle, which struck shortly before the March 7, 2023, Census date.
The report also found children and young people experiencing severe housing deprivation were more likely to have contact with the justice system as victims or alleged offenders, and were more likely to have parents who had been charged or imprisoned.
Fairfax New Zealand Limited
